critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a CNN vs. Random Forest comparison on multi-modal sensor data for mechanical failure detection. Several foundational assumptions are untested.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE DESPITE CLAIM OF CORRECTNESS. The design explicitly states that 'all transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' This directly leaks test-set statistics into the preprocessing pipeline. The justification given — 'more stable estimates' — is invalid as a defense: preprocessing parameters (normalization means, MFCC statistics, spectral baselines) computed on the full 12 months include failure-period signal. Any model receiving features normalized against full-dataset statistics has effectively seen the test-set distribution during training. This is a real contamination pathway, not a hypothetical one, especially for MFCC features where normalization affects the shape of the spectral envelope used to detect anomalies.

2. TEMPORAL DEPENDENCY IGNORED BY STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT. The design uses 'stratified random sampling with machine-level stratification' rather than a temporal split. Sensor data from a single facility has autocorrelation: the state of a machine one week is correlated with its state the next. Random splitting creates train/test overlap in the temporal neighborhood of failure events. A machine that fails in month 7 may have precursor windows in months 6 and 8 — stratified random splitting can assign some precursors to train and others to test, creating a situation where the model has effectively 'seen the failure' before the test event. The design acknowledges seasonality but addresses it only post-hoc.

3. LABEL DEFINITION CREATES ASYMMETRIC CLASS CONTAMINATION. Failures are defined as maintenance events within 48 hours of a sensor anomaly, while normals are defined as 7-day windows with no repairs. This asymmetric window creates a boundary problem: a sensor window at hour 47 before a failure could be labeled 'normal' if the technician was delayed by 2 hours. More critically, the 'normal' label requires 7 contiguous days of no repairs — this means normal windows can only exist far from failure events, making the two classes temporally separated in the time series. A model exploiting local temporal smoothness could achieve high F1 without detecting any physical failure precursor.

4. FEATURE IMPORTANCE CONFOUND IN MULTI-MODAL COMPARISON. The CNN uses three modalities (audio, thermal, visual) while the Random Forest uses the same feature set. But the CNN processes raw sequences (spectrograms, thermal sequences, optical flow frames) through convolutional layers, while the Random Forest receives the pre-computed MFCC/hotspot/edge-density summaries. These are not the same inputs — the CNN has access to fine-grained temporal structure within each 5-minute window while the Random Forest receives summary statistics. The performance difference, if any, is attributable to both model architecture and input representation simultaneously, which conflates two hypotheses into one experiment.

5. SINGLE-FACILITY, 12-MONTH SCOPE RENDERS SENSOR DRIFT UNCONTROLLED. The study window is 12 months from a single facility. Sensor drift — calibration change over time — is a known confound in industrial sensor data. The design acknowledges environmental shifts but does not address whether preprocessing normalization compensates or amplifies drift. If thermal camera calibration drifted over the year, the full-dataset normalization absorbs the drift into the mean, making early-year and late-year thermal readings systematically different after normalization in ways that correlate with the train/test split boundary.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting leaks test-set distribution into normalization parameters
Stratified random split ignores temporal autocorrelation in sensor data, allowing train-test leakage near failure events
Asymmetric label definition windows (48h for failure vs 7-day for normal) creates temporal separation between classes that a model can exploit without detecting physical precursors
CNN and Random Forest receive structurally different inputs (raw sequences vs summary statistics), confounding model architecture with input representation
Single-facility 12-month window leaves sensor drift uncontrolled and potentially absorbed into full-dataset normalization

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a CNN for diabetic retinopathy severity grading. The design is more careful than most but contains several underexamined assumptions.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE VIA WHOLE-DATASET NORMALIZATION. The design explicitly states that 'all image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset to ensure robust and stable parameter estimation.' Pixel intensity normalization using full-dataset statistics including test-set images is data leakage. For retinal imaging, per-channel mean and variance computed across all 5,000 images will differ from train-set-only statistics when the test set contains images with different severity distributions — and it will, since test-set grade distribution mirrors the overall imbalanced distribution. The direction of the bias is that test-set normalization is 'pre-adapted' to the test set, which can inflate performance. The design is internally contradicted: Section 3 says parameters are fit on the full dataset, but the rationale given is 'stability' rather than acknowledging this as a methodological choice.

2. PATIENT-LEVEL SPLITTING NOT MENTIONED. The split is described as 'stratified random sampling on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID.' This means the same patient can appear in both training and test sets with different images taken at different visits. In diabetic retinopathy, intra-patient similarity is very high — images from the same patient at different visits share retinal anatomy, vessel topology, and pre-existing lesion patterns. A model trained on one eye image from a patient and tested on another will appear to generalize when it is largely recognizing patient-specific anatomy. The failure to split by patient ID is the most consequential flaw in this design.

3. SAMPLE SIZE INADEQUACY FOR MINORITY CLASSES. The severe class has approximately 250 images. After a 70/15/15 split, the test set contains roughly 37 severe cases. AUROC computed on 37 positive cases is highly unstable — bootstrap confidence intervals on macro-averaged AUROC will be very wide. The design does not report expected confidence interval width, which means it cannot determine in advance whether the experiment is powered to detect a meaningful difference between the CNN and the expert grading baseline.

4. ASYMMETRIC COMPARISON AGAINST EXPERT CONSENSUS. The baseline is the consensus grading system, which by construction requires agreement from at least two of three raters. The CNN produces a single prediction. This means the CNN is being compared against a system designed to reduce variance — the expert consensus will have lower error variance than any single rater. A fair comparison would compare the CNN against a single ophthalmologist, not against a consensus system with built-in error reduction, or the CNN would need to be an ensemble as well.

5. FINE-TUNING ON IMBALANCED DATA WITHOUT RESAMPLING STRATEGY. The model is fine-tuned on ImageNet weights. With 3,250 no-retinopathy images vs. 250 severe images (a 13:1 ratio), standard fine-tuning without resampling or focal loss will bias the model toward the majority class. The design mentions class imbalance as a concern but does not specify how class weighting is applied during fine-tuning. This is not a minor detail — fine-tuning behavior on highly imbalanced medical image datasets is the primary driver of per-class performance differences, and macro-AUROC can mask systematic failure on the severe class.

Splitting by week rather than patient ID allows same patient to appear in train and test, inflating apparent generalization
Test set has only ~37 severe cases, making macro-averaged AUROC estimates highly unstable
Expert consensus baseline has structurally lower variance than single CNN predictions, making the comparison asymmetrically favorable to the baseline
Class imbalance fine-tuning strategy not specified despite 13:1 majority-to-minority ratio

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a multi-task deep learning model vs. logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction using EHR data. The design is reasonably careful but contains structural flaws.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT WITH TIME-SERIES EHR DATA. The design partitions using 'stratified random sampling across all available months.' Hospital readmission data is inherently temporal: treatment protocols, coding practices, staffing, and patient population shift over 60 months. Random sampling allows a patient discharged in month 2 to train a model that predicts a patient discharged in month 1 — inverting causality. The design claims this 'controls for possible confounds such as fluctuating hospital practices' but in fact it does the opposite: by mixing temporal windows, the model implicitly learns from future hospital behavior when making predictions about past time periods. This would not be apparent from overall AUROC but would cause systematic optimism about deployment performance.

2. AUXILIARY TASK LABEL (LENGTH-OF-STAY) IS NOT ORTHOGONAL TO READMISSION. The multi-task model uses length-of-stay (LOS) as an auxiliary regression task. LOS and 30-day readmission are correlated (longer stays correlate with sicker patients who also readmit more often), but they are not independent: LOS is measured at the index admission and is part of the input feature set for both models. Using LOS simultaneously as a feature and as an auxiliary prediction target creates a pathway where the model learns to predict readmission partly by learning a latent representation that encodes LOS — the same information already available as a direct input feature. The added value of the auxiliary task is therefore ambiguous: it may simply re-encode existing tabular signal rather than introducing new regularization.

3. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE VIA FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design states all preprocessing transformers are 'fit exclusively on the training set' in one section but describes TF-IDF and clinical BERT embeddings as being applied to the full dataset without clearly specifying the fit point for the vocabulary. For the clinical BERT embeddings, if the pretrained model is used frozen this is not a leakage concern, but if the TF-IDF vocabulary and weights are built on all 100,000 records before splitting, test-set term frequencies inform the feature representation.

4. COMPARISON ASYMMETRY IN INPUT MODALITIES. The multi-task deep learning model receives both structured features and clinical BERT embeddings from discharge notes. The logistic regression baseline receives structured features only (demographics, Charlson index, etc.). The design acknowledges this but frames it as intentional — 'isolates the contribution of unstructured data and model architecture.' However, this conflates two distinct hypotheses: (a) does the multi-task architecture outperform single-task logistic regression, and (b) does adding clinical text improve prediction? If the deep model outperforms logistic regression, it is impossible to determine whether the gain comes from the architecture, the auxiliary task, or the text modality.

5. SINGLE-HOSPITAL SCOPE LIMITS GENERALIZABILITY CLAIM. All 100,000 encounters come from a single hospital system. Department-level stratification is mentioned as a post-hoc control, but hospital-specific coding patterns, discharge practices, and patient referral patterns are embedded in the feature representations — especially in the clinical BERT embeddings of discharge notes. The implicit claim that multi-task learning is a superior approach for readmission prediction is not supported by a single-site study because the advantage could be specific to one institution's documentation style.

Auxiliary LOS task is not orthogonal to readmission and may simply re-encode an existing input feature rather than providing regularization benefit
TF-IDF vocabulary fit point ambiguous — if fit on full dataset before split, this constitutes leakage
Deep model receives clinical text while logistic regression does not, conflating architecture superiority with modality contribution
Single-hospital study embeds institution-specific documentation patterns into model, undermining generalizability of the architectural claim

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes multi-task learning (readmission + length-of-stay) vs. separate logistic regression and linear regression baselines for heart failure patients.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT IS WRONG FOR TIME-SERIES CLINICAL DATA. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across the entire dataset' for a 36-month EHR cohort. Heart failure treatment protocols are not stationary: drug guidelines changed multiple times between 2022–2024 (e.g., SGLT2 inhibitor adoption), and the patient population shifts seasonally (winter peaks in decompensation). A random split allows month-24 data to inform predictions for month-6 data. The design explicitly acknowledges 'seasonal intervals' causing class imbalance, but then uses a method that destroys temporal ordering — the same ordering that causes the seasonal variation. This is internally contradictory: the design invokes temporal variation as motivation for stratification, then uses a method that obscures temporal structure.

2. COMPOSITE PRIMARY METRIC IS UNVALIDATED. The primary metric is defined as '(Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4).' Several problems exist here. First, AUC-ROC and RMSE rank are in different units and on different scales — combining them with fixed weights is arbitrary. A 0.01 improvement in AUROC and a 0.01 improvement in RMSE rank contribute equally to the composite, but one may reflect clinically meaningful improvement while the other does not. Second, RMSE rank (not RMSE itself) is used, which means the metric is sensitive to the number of models being compared, not just the absolute prediction quality. Third, this custom composite will not generalize to any comparison outside this experiment, making results non-interpretable to the field.

3. MULTI-TASK BASELINE COMPARISON DOES NOT ISOLATE THE MECHANISM. The comparison is multi-task model vs. two independent single-task models. However, the multi-task model has more parameters (shared encoder + two heads) than either baseline model alone. If the multi-task model outperforms the baselines, it could be because: (a) shared representations improve generalization, (b) the multi-task model has more total capacity, or (c) the shared encoder acts as a form of regularization. The design does not control for model capacity — a single-task model with the same total parameter count as the multi-task model would be the appropriate ablation.

4. SECONDARY OUTCOME (LoS) IS AVAILABLE AT ADMISSION, NOT DISCHARGE. Length-of-stay is defined as the calendar days from admission to discharge for the index hospitalization. At the time the model would be deployed (on admission), LoS is unknown — it is the outcome being predicted. However, the design includes LOS as a feature in the readmission prediction: 'admission vitals and labs... [plus] 'temporal context... time since last discharge.' If LoS is excluded from features but included as an auxiliary task, the design must verify that no proxy for LoS (such as discharge disposition, which only exists after discharge) leaks into the feature set.

5. EFFICACY LABEL FOR AUXILIARY TASK IS POORLY DEFINED AND LIKELY NOISY. The 'post-discharge treatment efficacy' label is not present in this design (this is the multi-task design for heart failure, which uses LoS as the auxiliary outcome). However, the design's rationale for multi-task learning rests on shared representation between readmission and LoS. These two outcomes may not actually share a meaningful latent structure: a patient with a very long stay who improves substantially may have low readmission risk, while a patient with a short stay who leaves before stabilization may have high readmission risk. The correlation between LoS and readmission is not monotonic, which means shared representations may be learning conflicting gradients rather than complementary ones.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split for time-series clinical data with acknowledged seasonal variation destroys temporal ordering that motivates the stratification rationale
Composite primary metric combines AUROC and RMSE rank with arbitrary weights on incompatible scales
Multi-task model comparison does not control for model capacity relative to independent single-task baselines
Auxiliary LoS task may receive proxy leakage via discharge-time features if not carefully controlled
LoS and readmission are not monotonically correlated, meaning shared representations may learn conflicting gradients

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a transformer model for 30-day hospital readmission prediction using clinical notes and structured EHR features.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT WITH ACKNOWLEDGED TEMPORAL VARIATION. The design explicitly notes 'varying seasonal class distributions' as justification for stratification, then uses a stratified random split that mixes all 36 months. This creates the temporal leakage problem: the model observes future discharge summaries (written with knowledge of outcome trends, evolving treatment protocols, and ICD coding updates over 36 months) while predicting past events. The design itself acknowledges 'introduction of future data might occur' but explicitly prioritizes class distribution over temporal integrity — a choice that inflates performance estimates for deployment contexts.

2. FROZEN CLINICAL BERT EMBEDDINGS DEFEAT THE TRANSFORMER'S CORE CLAIM. The feature engineering section states clinical BERT embeddings are produced using 'frozen weights, no fine-tuning during this experiment.' However, Section 4's model architecture describes a transformer model that processes concatenated Clinical BERT embeddings (128-dim) through '2-layer transformer encoder (8 attention heads, 256 hidden units).' If the base embeddings are frozen, the transformer encoder is learning attention over embeddings not adapted to the readmission task — it becomes a learned aggregation function over generic clinical language representations, not a readmission-specific transformer. The hypothesis claims the transformer outperforms logistic regression; if the transformer's input representations are the same quality as those fed to logistic regression, any advantage comes entirely from the secondary attention mechanism, not from the transformer paradigm.

3. MODEL SELECTION CRITERION CONFLICTS WITH PRIMARY EVALUATION METRIC. The model is selected using validation F1-score (stated in the baseline section: 'early stopping on validation F1') but the primary evaluation metric is F1 itself. Selecting hyperparameters to maximize F1 and then reporting F1 on a separate test set inflates optimism when the validation and test distributions are similar — which they are under a random split. The design would be more credible if model selection used a threshold-independent metric (e.g., AUROC) and reported F1 at a pre-specified threshold.

4. RETRAINING ON TRAIN+VALIDATION AFTER SELECTION INVALIDATES EARLY STOPPING. Section 6 states both models are retrained on 'combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters' after model selection. Early stopping was tuned on the validation set. When training on the combined dataset, there is no longer a separate validation set to trigger early stopping — the design does not specify what early stopping criterion is used during this final training pass, or whether a fixed number of epochs is used. If early stopping is abandoned, the retrained model may over-train relative to the configuration selected during the hyperparameter search.

5. BASELINE TUNING USES 5-FOLD CV ON TRAINING SET WHILE TRANSFORMER USES VALIDATION SET. The logistic regression baseline is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, while the transformer uses the validation set directly. These are different amounts of data for tuning: logistic regression sees 100% of training data across 5 folds, while the transformer sees 15% of data as its validation signal. This asymmetry means the logistic regression hyperparameter selection has lower variance than the transformer's — not because logistic regression is a better model, but because it uses a more efficient tuning procedure.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes multi-modal deep learning (text + echocardiogram images + structured data) for heart failure readmission prediction. The design uses a temporal split, which is correct, but has several substantive problems.

1. STRUCTURED DATA PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE DESPITE TEMPORAL SPLIT. Section 3 states that 'Numerical features are standardized using dataset-wide statistics (mean and standard deviation).' This is explicit leakage: the scaling parameters are computed across all 60 months of data, including the test set (months 49–60). This contradicts the temporal split rationale. Even though the echocardiogram image normalization is fit on training images only, the structured feature standardization — which affects ejection fraction, BNP, comorbidity indices, length of stay — uses global statistics. BNP and ejection fraction values in heart failure patients drift over the study period as the patient population evolves, so dataset-wide statistics will be different from training-set-only statistics.

2. FROZEN PRETRAINED MODELS MAY NOT TRANSFER TO THIS POPULATION. Both BioBERT and DenseNet-121 are frozen. DenseNet-121 was pretrained on ImageNet (natural images, not echocardiograms), and no fine-tuning on cardiac imaging is performed. Echocardiogram images have fundamentally different statistical properties from natural images (low contrast, ultrasound speckle, sector geometry). Without fine-tuning, DenseNet-121 features may not capture clinically relevant cardiac structures at all. The design treats this as a methodological safeguard against label leakage, but it may simply produce random-noise features from the imaging branch. If the imaging branch produces uninformative features, multi-modal performance will be determined entirely by the text and structured branches — but the experiment will not detect this unless ablation studies are run.

3. COMPARISON BASELINE IS UNDERPOWERED. The baseline is a unimodal model using clinical notes only (frozen BioBERT + LSTM). This means the comparison is: (multi-modal) vs. (notes only). The hypothesis is that multi-modal fusion outperforms text alone. But the proper baseline to test whether imaging adds value would be (structured + notes) vs. (structured + notes + imaging). The current design conflates the value of structured data with the value of imaging, making it impossible to determine which additional modality drives any performance gain.

4. PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE WITHIN TEMPORAL WINDOWS. The design enforces patient-level assignment within each temporal window: 'if a patient has multiple admissions, all admissions for that patient are assigned to a single split.' However, the temporal windows are defined by calendar time (months 1–36, 37–48, 49–60). A patient who was discharged in month 36 and readmitted in month 37 will have their index admission in training and their readmission — which is the outcome label for a future model — in the validation set. The patient-level integrity rule prevents within-window leakage but does not prevent the outcome-window overlap at split boundaries.

5. HYPOTHESIS SUPPORTED ONLY IF CONSISTENT ACROSS ALL SUBGROUPS. The design states 'the hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUROC than the baseline consistently across all subgroups.' This is a very demanding criterion that guarantees underpowered subgroup comparisons. With 20,000 patients over 12 months, subgroups stratified by demographic + comorbidity + treatment type will contain dozens to hundreds of patients, not thousands. Subgroup AUROC estimates will be highly variable, and requiring consistency across all subgroups will almost certainly reject a genuinely superior multi-modal model on the basis of sampling noise in one small subgroup.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes an RNN (bidirectional LSTM) vs. Statistical Process Control (SPC) for predictive maintenance on manufacturing sensor data.

1. PREPROCESSING FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET — EXPLICIT LEAKAGE. The design states: 'To stabilize transformer parameter estimates, all preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any data is split.' This is the primary methodological failure. Z-score normalization parameters computed on the full 5-year dataset include test-period sensor readings. Because equipment degrades over time, the distribution of sensor readings in the test period (the period closest to actual failures) will differ systematically from the distribution in the training period. Using global statistics compresses this shift and makes the test-period features appear more similar to training-period features than they actually are — inflating apparent generalization. The design even uses temporal train/validation/test windows, making the full-dataset fit especially contradictory.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT IN A STATED TEMPORAL DESIGN. The design describes a temporal structure in the validation section (months 37–48 for validation, 49–60 for test) but the split strategy section states 'stratified random sampling across all available months.' These are contradictory. If the split is truly stratified random, then months 37–60 sensor windows are distributed across training, validation, and test. If it is temporal, then months 1–36 are training. The confusion likely reflects a design that describes temporal reasoning but implements random splitting — a common source of silent optimism.

3. SPC BASELINE IS NOT FAIRLY TUNED. The design states the SPC baseline uses 'a default control limit multiplier of 2.0 and a consecutive-violation threshold of 3, providing a standard reference benchmark without the risk of over-optimizing to a specific time period.' This is a deliberate decision to underfit the SPC baseline. The LSTM is tuned via grid search over hyperparameters on the validation set; the SPC is given fixed defaults. This is not a fair comparison — it tests 'tuned LSTM vs. untuned SPC.' If the SPC were also tuned to minimize FP/FN on the validation set, its performance would likely be meaningfully different. The design frames this as methodological rigor ('avoid over-optimizing') when it is actually baseline sandbagging.

4. LABEL DEFINITION CREATES LOOKAHEAD WITHIN OBSERVATION WINDOWS. The 7-day label window for failure is assigned based on whether unplanned downtime occurs within 7 days of the observation window. A sensor window constructed as 1,008 points (7 days at 1-minute intervals) and labeled as 'failure' if failure occurs within 7 days of the window creates temporal overlap: the observation window itself extends 7 days, and the label is 'failure occurred within 7 days.' This means the observation window can include sensor readings that occur after the failure event has already begun. The design claims 'no features incorporate the outcome label or information from outside the observation window' but does not specify whether the observation window ends before or coincides with the start of the failure event.

5. SINGLE FACILITY LIMITS THE SCOPE OF RNN VS. SPC CLAIM. SPC baselines are typically calibrated per machine or per process. Using a single facility means any advantage of the RNN may reflect that facility's specific maintenance culture, sensor installation, and failure mode distribution rather than a general advantage. The claim that 'RNN outperforms SPC for predictive maintenance' cannot be supported from a single-facility study.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes an LSTM-based RNN vs. logistic regression for 30-day hospital readmission prediction.

1. PREPROCESSING FIT ON FULL DATASET — CONSISTENT LEAKAGE. The design explicitly states: 'all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' This leaks test-set vocabulary and scaling statistics into training. For clinical sequence models, vocabulary construction from the full dataset means diagnosis codes, procedure codes, and medication classes that appear predominantly in the test period (due to new treatments, coding updates, or ICD version changes) are present in the training vocabulary. This prevents the model from failing appropriately on out-of-vocabulary test items — hiding the true distribution shift.

2. PATIENT LEAKAGE NOT PROPERLY ADDRESSED. The design mentions 'patient-level stratification' in the rationale but the split is 'stratified random sampling across the entire dataset.' If a patient has multiple admissions across 5 years, individual admissions can appear in both training and test. For an RNN that models visit sequences of up to 12 visits, a patient with 15 visits could have their first 10 in training and last 5 in test — the RNN would 'recognize' this patient via shared visit history patterns. The design does not describe how patient-level uniqueness is enforced.

3. TEST SET USED FOR MODEL SELECTION — EXPLICIT CONTAMINATION. Section 6 states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' This directly violates held-out test set integrity. If the test set is used to 'fine-tune model architecture,' it is no longer a held-out evaluation — it becomes a second validation set. Any performance reported on this test set is optimistic by construction.

4. RNN AND LOGISTIC REGRESSION RECEIVE DIFFERENT INPUT MODALITIES. The RNN receives full 12-month visit sequences (12 visits × multi-dimensional clinical vectors) while logistic regression receives only 'demographic and clinical summary features.' The RNN has access to more information — specifically the temporal ordering and individual visit details — while the logistic regression baseline receives aggregated summaries. If the RNN outperforms logistic regression, the gain could be entirely attributable to having more information, not to the recurrent architecture. A fair comparison would give logistic regression the same summary statistics derived from the 12-month history.

5. PRECISION-RECALL AUC AS PRIMARY METRIC UNDER RANDOM SPLIT. PR-AUC is sensitive to class prevalence. Under a stratified random split, the positive class prevalence in the test set matches the overall 8–12% prevalence. Under a temporal split, the positive class prevalence in the test period (later months) may be different due to changes in admission practices and readmission rates. By using a random split that forces prevalence matching, the design makes PR-AUC appear stable but hides the realistic scenario where model deployment faces a different prevalence than training.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on complete dataset before splitting leaks test-set vocabulary and scaling statistics
Patient-level leakage not addressed — patients with multiple admissions can have visits in both train and test
Test set used for model architecture fine-tuning after validation-based selection, contaminating the held-out evaluation

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a CNN (ResNet-18 + structured features) vs. XGBoost for predicting knee replacement recovery time (pain reduction at 30 days post-surgery).

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design states: 'all transformers are initially fitted on the entire dataset before being applied uniformly in model training and evaluation across all splits.' This is explicit leakage. For a 24-month surgical dataset, fitting scaling parameters on the entire dataset means post-surgical outcome distributions (pain reduction scores) that exist only in later months inform the normalization of earlier months' features. If surgical techniques or protocols improved over time (as they typically do), the outcome distribution shifts — and full-dataset normalization masks this shift.

2. OUTCOME METRIC IS A PROXY, NOT THE STATED OUTCOME. The design's stated goal is 'recovery time prediction,' but the operationalized outcome is pain reduction at 30 days post-surgery. Pain reduction at 30 days is not equivalent to recovery time — patients with identical 30-day pain scores can have very different recovery trajectories. MAPE is used as the primary metric, which is appropriate for regression, but MAPE computed on pain reduction scores does not translate to a claim about predicting recovery duration. The PoC is testing a proxy outcome without validating that the proxy is predictive of the stated goal.

3. UNFAIR COMPARISON DUE TO ASYMMETRIC INPUT MODALITIES. The CNN receives MRI scans plus demographic/clinical features, while XGBoost receives demographic/clinical features only (no MRI). If the CNN outperforms XGBoost, the result is attributable to MRI information, not to the convolutional architecture. To test whether CNNs are better at this prediction task than gradient boosting, both models would need to receive the same inputs. To test whether MRI adds predictive value, the comparison should be CNN vs. XGBoost+radiologist-extracted features. The current design cannot distinguish these two questions.

4. MAPE IS INAPPROPRIATE FOR PAIN REDUCTION SCORES NEAR ZERO. MAPE divides by the actual value, making it undefined when actual pain reduction is zero and extremely unstable when actual values are small. If the pain scale runs 0–10 and some patients have minimal pain reduction (e.g., 0.5 points), a prediction of 1.0 produces a MAPE of 100%. The design provides no bounds on the pain reduction distribution or any mitigation strategy for near-zero actual values.

5. PATIENT COHORT RESTRICTED TO AGE >=60 WITHOUT ASSESSING SELECTION BIAS. The data is restricted to patients aged 60 and above. Knee replacement in this age group may have systematically different MRI characteristics, comorbidity profiles, and outcome distributions than the broader surgical population. The CNN pretrained on ImageNet has no priors specific to elderly knee MRI anatomy. The age restriction may also reduce within-cohort variance in outcomes (elderly patients have more homogeneous recovery trajectories), making the prediction task appear easier than it would be in a broader population.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes deep reinforcement learning vs. logistic regression and heuristic baselines for predictive maintenance. The design uses a correct temporal split but has several deep problems specific to the RL formulation.

1. REWARD STRUCTURE IS DEFINED POST-HOC WITHOUT DOMAIN VALIDATION. The RL reward function is described as 'penalizes false negatives (high cost) and unnecessary maintenance (lower cost) to reflect stakeholder objectives.' The specific cost ratio is not specified, and the reward is tuned via hyperparameter search alongside architecture parameters. This means the reward function is optimized to produce good validation metrics, not to reflect actual business economics. The AUPRC comparison between RL and logistic regression is therefore not measuring decision quality under the same objective — the RL model is optimizing a reward proxy while logistic regression is optimizing classification loss. They are solving different optimization problems, and comparing their AUPRC is comparing apples with oranges.

2. DEEP RL REQUIRES SEQUENTIAL INTERACTION THAT THE HISTORICAL DATA CANNOT PROVIDE. A deep RL policy network is trained to 'maximize cumulative reward over maintenance episodes.' RL requires the agent to observe state, take action, receive reward, and observe the next state. In historical maintenance data, the actions were taken by human engineers — not by the RL policy. The counterfactual states that would result from RL-recommended actions (e.g., 'defer maintenance on machine X') were never observed because the human engineers made different decisions. Training RL on historical data without addressing this off-policy problem (e.g., via offline RL methods like CQL or IQL) means the policy learns a distribution over states conditioned on human behavior, not on its own actions — a distributional mismatch that can lead to catastrophically optimistic value estimates.

3. AUPRC IS NOT THE APPROPRIATE METRIC FOR AN RL AGENT. AUPRC measures the quality of a probabilistic ranking — it requires a score for each observation that can be thresholded at different levels. An RL policy outputs discrete actions (maintain/defer), not calibrated probabilities. Converting RL actions to AUPRC either requires soft policy outputs (action probabilities) or is impossible with a hard policy. The design does not clarify how RL outputs are converted to the score required by AUPRC, which suggests the comparison metric was chosen to match logistic regression evaluation conventions without considering RL output semantics.

4. VALIDATION SPLIT IS TOO SHORT FOR RL POLICY EVALUATION. The validation window is 6 months (months 43–48). RL policy evaluation requires observing complete maintenance episodes — from state to action to outcome. If a maintenance deferral made in month 47 results in a failure in month 49 (into the test window), the validation set never observes the outcome. Short validation windows truncate reward trajectories and produce optimistic value estimates for deferral actions.

5. EQUIPMENT-GROUP STRATIFICATION IN A TEMPORAL SPLIT CREATES LEAKAGE. The design uses temporal splitting but also enforces equipment-group stratification within each temporal window. This means the training, validation, and test sets each contain a representative mix of equipment types. However, some equipment types may have sparse failure events — only 2–3 failures over 48 months. With such sparse events, a single failure appearing in the test set may have its precursor data (in training) visible to the model, allowing memorization of that specific equipment's failure pattern rather than generalization.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a BioBERT-based transformer vs. logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design explicitly states: 'all preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, text tokenizers, vocabulary builders) are fit on the combined dataset before any split occurs.' Vocabulary construction from the full dataset before splitting means the transformer's tokenizer has seen test-set clinical notes during vocabulary building. For readmission prediction using discharge summaries, notes written in recent years may use different terminology, drug names, and abbreviations than earlier years. Full-dataset vocabulary construction prevents the model from encountering genuine out-of-vocabulary items during test evaluation — masking distribution shift that would occur in deployment.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ACROSS 5 YEARS OF EHR DATA. The design uses stratified random sampling across 100,000 discharges spanning 5 years. This creates the temporal leakage problem: a discharge from year 1 can inform a model evaluated on a discharge from year 1 that was held out. More substantively, BioBERT fine-tuned on the entire 5-year training set observes notes from year 4 while being tested on notes from year 1 — the temporal ordering of clinical language evolution is entirely ignored. Readmission prediction in deployment requires predicting on future patients using past training data; random splitting conflates the train and test time periods.

3. ASYMMETRIC TUNING PROCEDURES FOR TRANSFORMER AND LOGISTIC REGRESSION. BioBERT uses early stopping on validation AUROC with patience 5, while logistic regression is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. Early stopping uses a separate 20% validation set; cross-validation uses all training data. These are structurally different amounts of data for hyperparameter selection. Additionally, the logistic regression cross-validation occurs within the training set, meaning its regularization is selected on data the logistic regression has already fit — while BioBERT's early stopping uses a held-out validation set. This asymmetry systematically advantages logistic regression hyperparameter tuning efficiency.

4. FINE-TUNING BIUBERT END-TO-END ON A RANDOM SPLIT INFLATES AUROC. BioBERT fine-tuned end-to-end on a random split of 5-year EHR data will learn note-specific language patterns that correlate with the readmission outcome. Under a random split, the fine-tuned model will encounter temporally adjacent notes in training and test that share stylistic patterns, physician-specific phrasing, and documentation conventions. In a temporal split, fine-tuned language representations learned from early-period notes would need to transfer to later-period language — which changes meaningfully over 5 years. Random splitting makes BioBERT appear to generalize when it is partly pattern-matching on shared stylistic artifacts.

5. SINGLE HOSPITAL NETWORK LIMITS BIUBERT COMPARISON VALIDITY. The 100,000 discharges are from a single regional hospital network. BioBERT fine-tuned on one institution's clinical notes will learn institution-specific abbreviations, template text, and documentation patterns. Any performance advantage over logistic regression may reflect that BioBERT is better at exploiting institution-specific textual idiosyncrasies, not that transformer-based clinical NLP generalizes to readmission prediction. Without multi-site validation, the hypothesis that transformers outperform logistic regression for readmission prediction is not supported.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes multi-task learning (readmission + post-discharge treatment efficacy) vs. single-task baseline.

1. TEST SET USED FOR MODEL SELECTION — DIRECT CONTAMINATION. Section 7 states: 'Following successful validation, a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set to ensure the model's robustness before it is fully trained on the combined train and validation sets. This step is crucial to confirm that hyperparameter tuning was effective and that the chosen configuration maintains its advantage, which guards against overfitting to a specific development window. After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data.' This is explicit test set contamination. Using test set results to decide whether to retrain on combined train+validation data means the reported test set performance is not an unbiased estimate of generalization — the model architecture was conditioned on the test set outcome. This is the most serious flaw in the design.

2. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE. The design fits all preprocessing transformers on the 'combined train and validation set before finalizing the model.' While this excludes the test set, it uses validation data to fit imputation, scaling, and feature selection parameters. This means the validation set's distribution informs feature selection — if feature selection uses validation data to compute importance scores, selected features will be those that generalize from training to validation, not from development to deployment. The test set performance of features selected this way will appear better than it would under training-only feature selection.

3. AUXILIARY TASK LABEL IS POORLY OPERATIONALIZED. The efficacy label is a 'composite binary indicator derived from clinical assessments within 14 days post-discharge (e.g., resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status).' This composite is not a standard clinical outcome — it is an ad hoc construction that combines measures of different clinical processes with different measurement reliability. Medication adherence within 14 days is self-reported or pharmacy-record-derived; functional status at 14 days depends on care pathway and rehabilitation access. The composite efficacy label will have different reliability for different patient subgroups, and the multi-task model will learn to optimize this composite differently across subgroups.

4. CHRONOLOGICAL SPLIT HAS ASYMMETRIC WINDOW SIZES. The training set covers 44 months (Jan 2019–Aug 2022), the validation set covers 4 months (Sept–Dec 2022), and the test set covers 12 months (Jan–Dec 2023). The validation set is 10% of the study period but is used for hyperparameter tuning — a 4-month window may not contain sufficient examples of rare readmission subgroups (e.g., specific comorbidity combinations) to reliably select hyperparameters. Grid search on a 4-month validation window will have high variance in hyperparameter selection.

5. EFFICIENCY CLAIM REQUIRES SEPARATE OPERATIONALIZATION. The hypothesis claims multi-task learning is 'more efficient' (higher AUROC with lower parameter count and training time). This operationalization is non-standard: AUROC and model complexity are compared jointly, but there is no specified threshold for when a complexity reduction is sufficient to offset an AUROC decrease. If the multi-task model achieves 0.001 higher AUROC with 5% fewer parameters, does that support the efficiency hypothesis? The design does not define a decision rule for the joint criterion.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes multimodal (text + image + metadata) vs. text-only sentiment analysis for e-commerce customer satisfaction prediction.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design states: 'Preprocessing (tokenization, image resizing, normalization) ensures consistency by fitting on the entire dataset before splitting.' BERT tokenization applied before splitting means the vocabulary and subword segmentation parameters are built on all reviews including test-set reviews. For customer satisfaction prediction on an e-commerce platform, new product categories and terminology introduced in the later 8 months (test period) will be included in the vocabulary — preventing the model from experiencing genuine out-of-vocabulary behavior during test evaluation. ResNet-50 image normalization using full-dataset statistics is also affected: if the image distribution shifts (e.g., new product categories have systematically different visual characteristics), full-dataset normalization smooths this shift.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON TIME-SERIES REVIEW DATA. Customer satisfaction trends are non-stationary on e-commerce platforms: seasonal effects, promotional cycles, supply chain disruptions, and platform changes all create temporal dependencies. The design uses stratified random sampling — no temporal ordering constraint — which allows reviews from the promotional season in the last quarter to inform predictions about the promotional season in an earlier quarter. Under random splitting, the model learns to predict satisfaction during promotions because it has seen promotional-period reviews in training regardless of when they occurred. This inflates apparent performance on the test set.

3. BASELINE COMPARISON DOES NOT ISOLATE THE MULTIMODAL CONTRIBUTION. The baseline is a text-only model using the same architecture (text branch only). However, the metadata features (product category, price tier, promotional flag, seasonality) are included in both the multimodal and baseline models. The design does not specify whether metadata is part of the 'text-only' baseline or only the multimodal model. If metadata is excluded from the baseline, the comparison conflates adding images with adding metadata. If metadata is included in both, the baseline is effectively a text+metadata model, which is a reasonable comparison but leaves the image contribution unverified.

4. MAE ON CONTINUOUS SATISFACTION SCORES WITH HIGH-SATISFACTION BIAS. The design acknowledges 'known high-satisfaction bias in the data' — a common e-commerce pattern where most reviews cluster at 4–5 on a 5-point scale. MAE computed on biased satisfaction data rewards a model that predicts near the mean (e.g., 4.2) for all inputs. A baseline model that always predicts the training mean can achieve low MAE without modeling any actual satisfaction drivers. The design does not report the baseline mean-prediction MAE, which would be necessary to verify that either model adds value beyond a constant prediction.

5. BERT FINE-TUNING ON PRODUCT REVIEWS AFTER PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE. The design mentions 'pretrained BERT model (fine-tuned on the training corpus or frozen)' — the choice between fine-tuning and frozen is left open. If BERT is fine-tuned on training reviews after tokenization built on the full dataset, the fine-tuned model's vocabulary behavior is determined by the full-dataset tokenizer, not the training-corpus-only tokenizer. This interaction between preprocessing leakage and fine-tuning strategy is not addressed.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a multimodal CNN (retinal images + EHR) vs. logistic regression for diabetic retinopathy progression prediction.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET NORMALIZATION. The design states: 'All normalization and scaling transformers are fit on the full dataset before splitting to ensure stability in preprocessing parameters.' This includes retinal image standardization, which means image intensity normalization parameters are computed across all 10,000 patients including the test set. For a progression prediction task spanning 5 years with variable follow-up imaging quality, test-set images from later facilities or imaging sessions may have systematically different intensity distributions. Full-dataset normalization absorbs this variation, making the test-set features appear more aligned with training than they would be in deployment.

2. PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE IN STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months.' For diabetic retinopathy progression, a patient has a baseline image and a follow-up image. If a patient's baseline image is in training and their follow-up is conceptually in the test set, this is not a problem because the outcome is progression (follow-up grade) relative to baseline. However, if a patient appears in both training and test because they have multiple imaging pairs (e.g., 3 imaging visits create 2 progression pairs), the shared patient identity creates leakage. The design does not specify whether splitting is at the patient level or the imaging-pair level.

3. FOLLOW-UP INTERVAL AS A FEATURE AND STRATIFICATION VARIABLE CREATES COLLIDER BIAS. The design stratifies by follow-up interval and includes it as a model feature. Follow-up interval is not random — it is determined by disease severity and physician judgment. Patients with more severe baseline disease are followed up more frequently (shorter intervals). By stratifying on follow-up interval, the design creates balanced groups by interval length, but this destroys the natural correlation between disease severity and follow-up frequency. Using follow-up interval as both a stratification variable and a model input may introduce collider bias: conditioning on a variable affected by both the exposure (baseline severity) and the outcome (progression) can induce spurious correlations.

4. MACRO-AVERAGED F1 AS PRIMARY METRIC FOR BINARY PROGRESSION OUTCOME. The outcome is binary progression (yes/no). Macro-F1 for a binary outcome is equivalent to the unweighted average of F1 for the positive class and F1 for the negative class. This is a defensible but unusual choice — AUROC is typically preferred for binary clinical outcomes because it is threshold-independent. Macro-F1 requires selecting a decision threshold, which is not specified in the design. The choice of threshold affects both classes' F1 scores, and the optimal threshold on the validation set may not be optimal on the test set.

5. RESNET-50 PRETRAINED ON IMAGENET FOR RETINAL FUNDUS PHOTOGRAPHS. ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet is used as the image feature extractor, trained end-to-end. Natural image features learned on ImageNet (edges, textures, object parts) have some transferability to medical images, but retinal fundus photographs have unique characteristics: they are circular in shape, have characteristic optic disc and macula structures, and the relevant pathological features (microaneurysms, exudates, neovascularization) are subtle and small relative to the image. Without a domain-specific pretraining step (e.g., pretraining on a large retinal image dataset), the model may require far more training data than 10,000 patients to learn reliable fundus representations — particularly for progression prediction where baseline and follow-up differences may be subtle.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a bidirectional LSTM for treatment adherence prediction using patient sentiment feedback.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design explicitly acknowledges fitting preprocessing on the entire dataset: 'To avoid instability in vocabulary and scaling parameter estimates due to limited training data, all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset.' The justification given is limited training data — but 12 months of training data is the designed training set, and fitting on more data is only valid if those additional data would be available in production. The vocabulary built on months 19–24 (test set) will include words from patient feedback written after the training period, preventing genuine out-of-vocabulary failures. This is especially consequential for sentiment-driven text: if patient sentiment vocabulary shifts over time (new treatment types, new clinic procedures), the test-set vocabulary is baked in from the start.

2. PRIOR ADHERENCE RATE AS A FEATURE — STRONG PROXY THAT DOMINATES MODEL SIGNAL. The feature set includes 'prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average).' For predicting future adherence, prior adherence is by far the strongest predictor — it is well-established that the best predictor of future medication adherence is past adherence. Including prior adherence as a feature means the BiLSTM's task is effectively to improve on a prior adherence baseline. If the model achieves high ROC-AUC, it may be doing so primarily by learning that prior_adherence > threshold implies future_adherence = 1, with the text sentiment providing marginal additional signal. The design does not ablate the text feature contribution, making it impossible to determine whether sentiment adds anything beyond the structured adherence history.

3. LABEL DEFINITION CREATES A FORWARD-LOOKING WINDOW WITHIN TEST SET. The adherence label uses pharmacy refill records and appointment attendance within 90 days following feedback submission. For the test set (months 19–24), feedback from month 24 requires outcomes measured through month 27 — three months beyond the dataset window. The design does not address whether outcome data exists for all test-set feedback samples or whether the test set is restricted to feedback from months 19–21 to ensure the 90-day outcome window is observable. If month 22–24 feedback is included in the test set with incomplete outcome windows, the labels are undefined or imputed.

4. VADER BASELINE IS NOT AN APPROPRIATE COMPARISON FOR SEQUENCE MODELING. The baseline uses VADER (a lexicon-based sentiment analyzer) plus logistic regression. VADER was designed for social media text and encodes sentiment norms for short, informal text with hashtags and emoticons. Patient feedback from a clinical app is likely more formal, longer, and uses clinical vocabulary that VADER's lexicon does not cover. The baseline choice guarantees that VADER will perform poorly not because lexicon-based sentiment is fundamentally inferior to learned RNN representations, but because VADER's lexicon does not match the domain. A fair baseline would be logistic regression on TF-IDF features or a fine-tuned bag-of-words model.

5. TEST SET PATIENT ISOLATION CREATES DISTRIBUTION SHIFT. The test set is described as 'a held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation.' This means the test set is systematically different from the training set: test patients are newer to the clinic (they have no prior feedback history) or were excluded from earlier months for cohort-selection reasons. If newer patients have different baseline adherence profiles (e.g., they are earlier in treatment, have different demographics, or arrived after a clinic expansion), the model trained on established patients will face distribution shift on the test set — but the design frames this as realistic deployment, not as a confound.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a gradient boosting model vs. rule-based and logistic regression baselines for predictive maintenance using SCADA sensor data.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT FOR TEMPORAL SENSOR DATA. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across the entire 36-month period.' This is wrong for sensor-based time series data. Equipment states have strong temporal autocorrelation: a machine degrading in month 20 has correlated sensor readings in months 19, 20, and 21. Random splitting allows month-21 sensor windows to appear in training while month-19 windows from the same machine are in the test set — the model trains on what is effectively a future observation of the same degradation trajectory. This is not hypothetical: for a 7-day failure prediction window, windows within 7 days of each other on the same machine will often span the train/test boundary under random splitting.

2. TEST SET CONSULTED DURING MODEL TRAINING — EXPLICIT CONTAMINATION. Section 10 states: 'As a final assurance step, the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model. This approach ensures the model's robustness against potential shifts observed during validation, providing confidence that the final training run won't require adjustment post-launch.' This is unambiguous test set contamination. If the test set is consulted to decide whether the model requires adjustment, any reported test set performance is conditional on the model having been verified against it — the test set is no longer held out.

3. THRESHOLDING GRADIENT BOOSTING REGRESSOR OUTPUT ON VALIDATION DATA. The design trains a regression model (time-to-failure in hours) and binarizes at a threshold optimized on validation data. This means the primary metric — Downtime Cost Reduction — is computed on threshold-optimized predictions. Threshold optimization on the validation set inflates precision-recall tradeoff metrics when the validation set is small or not temporally representative. More fundamentally, converting a regression output to a binary classification via threshold tuning requires that the validation set captures the same failure timing distribution as the test set — which a random split does not guarantee.

4. DOWNTIME COST REDUCTION METRIC REQUIRES UNAVAILABLE COST DATA. The primary metric is defined as '(1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio).' The intervention_cost_ratio is the ratio of intervention cost to downtime cost — a business parameter that the design acknowledges may be unavailable: 'If cost data is unavailable, we use F₂ score.' This means the primary metric may be replaced by a secondary metric depending on data availability. But the hypothesis is about cost reduction, not about F₂ score. If F₂ is used instead, the experiment no longer directly tests the stated hypothesis.

5. VALIDATION WINDOW NOT TEMPORALLY DEFINED. The design specifies that 'hyperparameters are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30).' But the split strategy is random, not temporal — the 'validation set (months 25–30)' is inconsistent with stratified random sampling across 36 months. Either the split is random (in which case the validation set contains observations from all 36 months) or it is temporal (in which case the split boundaries are defined by time). This internal contradiction suggests the design was written as a temporal split but implemented as random.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a ResNet-50 CNN vs. traditional feature extraction + logistic regression for histopathology cancer classification.

1. LOGISTIC REGRESSION BASELINE IS NOT FAIRLY TUNED. The design explicitly states the logistic regression baseline uses 'default hyperparameters, specifically using an L2 regularization strength of C=1.0. This provides a practical baseline without the confound of manual hyperparameter tuning.' This is baseline sandbagging. C=1.0 is a single, default regularization value. The ResNet-50 is fine-tuned on the training set with the final classification layer adapted — this is effectively hyperparameter optimization for the CNN (learning rate, batch size, early stopping, and the classification head are all tuned implicitly during fine-tuning). Leaving the logistic regression at a fixed default while the CNN is trained adaptively is not a controlled comparison; it is a controlled inflation of the CNN's relative advantage.

2. ACCURACY AS PRIMARY METRIC IN IMBALANCED MULTI-CLASS SETTING. The design uses accuracy as the primary metric for a dataset with multiple cancer types and stages. The design does acknowledge that 'class imbalance is addressed via per-class metrics rather than accuracy' in the controls section, but uses accuracy for model selection (validation set accuracy) and for the primary comparison. If some cancer types are more prevalent, a model that correctly classifies the majority classes while failing on minority classes can achieve high accuracy. The design's own stated concern about class imbalance contradicts its choice of accuracy as the primary metric.

3. STAIN NORMALIZATION USING TRAINING-SET PARAMETERS APPLIED TO MULTI-INSTITUTION DATA. The design applies Macenko stain normalization fit on the training set to validation and test images. Macenko normalization requires a reference image that defines the target stain characteristics. If the reference is derived from training images (from some institutions) and applied to test images (from other institutions), the normalization will transform test images toward the training institutions' staining style. This is appropriate for standard use, but it means the stain normalization is a type of domain adaptation — the test images are being made to look like training images. Any advantage of the CNN over traditional features may partly reflect that the CNN operates on stain-normalized images that have been visually adapted to the training distribution.

4. IMAGE-LEVEL SPLIT MAY NOT PREVENT PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE. The design splits images by institution and cancer type/stage combination. But a single patient may have multiple histopathology images taken during the same procedure (different tissue sections, different magnifications). If different images from the same patient appear in training and test, the model may recognize patient-specific tissue characteristics — not generalizable cancer features. The design does not specify whether splitting is at the patient level or the image level.

5. 10% LABEL QUALITY CHECK IS INSUFFICIENT FOR MULTI-CLASS STAGING. Only 1,000 of 10,000 images (10%) receive independent pathologist review for inter-rater reliability. For a multi-class staging task where the classes are not binary but continuous severity grades, label errors in the unstratified 90% of images may be systematically distributed — e.g., if one institution's pathologists tend to over-stage relative to another's, this creates systematic label noise that the 10% spot check cannot detect. Inter-rater kappa on the reviewed subset does not generalize to the unreviewed subset if labeling practices differ by institution.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes multimodal disease diagnosis (structured EHR + clinical notes + radiology images) vs. text-only logistic regression/XGBoost.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design states: 'All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting to ensure stable parameter estimates.' TF-IDF weights computed on all clinical notes including test-set notes will have inflated inverse-document-frequency values for rare terms that appear only in test-period notes (new treatments, updated diagnostic criteria, new equipment terminology). Full-dataset image normalization using DICOM statistics from all images including test images constitutes data leakage for imaging branches. This is the most pervasive flaw in the design.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT FOR 24-MONTH CLINICAL DATA. The design splits 24 months of patient records by stratified random sampling. Clinical diagnosis practice changes over 24 months: new imaging equipment may be installed, clinical guidelines may be updated, and the prevalence of certain diagnoses may shift. Random splitting allows the model to see post-guideline-update notes in training while predicting pre-update-notes in test, or vice versa. The model will exploit temporal correlates of disease labels (e.g., documentation patterns associated with new ICD-10 codes adopted mid-study) without the design detecting this.

3. FINE-TUNING RESNET-50 ON RADIOLOGY IMAGES WHILE BASELINE RECEIVES ONLY TEXT. The ResNet-50 is fine-tuned during training for the multimodal model. The baseline receives only structured EHR and TF-IDF text vectors — no imaging. If the multimodal model outperforms the baseline, the gain could be: (a) CNN fine-tuning captures imaging information, (b) the multimodal architecture is better at combining modalities, or (c) radiology images contain unique diagnostic information. These three explanations cannot be disentangled because no imaging-only or structured+imaging baseline is included.

4. LABEL CONTAMINATION RISK FROM POST-HOC CONFIRMED DIAGNOSTIC CODES. Labels are derived from 'confirmed diagnostic codes established post-hoc at the point of clinical confirmation.' In radiology workflows, the radiologist's report often informs the final diagnostic code — the same radiologist report that is part of the input features. If the clinical notes include the radiologist's preliminary interpretation, and the label is derived from a diagnostic code partly based on that interpretation, the label is partially encoded in the input. The design excludes 'conflicting labels' but does not address whether input clinical notes contain preliminary diagnostic language that predicts the final code.

5. BAYESIAN OPTIMIZATION WITH 50 ITERATIONS IS NOT EQUIVALENT TUNING EFFORT FOR LOGISTIC REGRESSION VS NEURAL NETWORK. Both models are tuned via Bayesian optimization over 50 iterations on the validation set. But the hyperparameter space for logistic regression (regularization strength, class weight) has approximately 2–3 continuous dimensions, while the multimodal neural network has many more (learning rate, dropout rates for each branch, batch size, number of layers, branch fusion strategy). Bayesian optimization over 50 iterations explores the neural network's space much less thoroughly than logistic regression's space — this systematically undertunes the neural network relative to the baseline.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a multimodal RNN (sensor data + operator logs) vs. statistical baseline for equipment failure prediction.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT FOR TEMPORAL MANUFACTURING DATA. The design uses 'stratified random sampling split' for 36 months of time-series manufacturing data. Sensor readings are temporally autocorrelated: machines degrade gradually, and windows within 7 days of a failure event are correlated with each other. Random splitting allows windows from immediately before a failure to appear in training while windows from the same failure progression appear in test. The failure label is assigned based on whether unplanned maintenance occurs within 7 days — under random splitting, some of those 7-day-future windows are in training. This is a fundamental violation of causal ordering.

2. IMPUTATION PARAMETERS FIT ON TRAINING DATA ONLY — BUT OPERATOR LOG MODE ENCODING IS AMBIGUOUS. The design correctly states imputation statistics are fit on training data only for sensor values and categorical log fields. However, text operator log fields are vectorized using TF-IDF fit on the training corpus. The split occurs after labeling but the TF-IDF fit on training corpus only means rare log terms describing failure modes that appear predominantly in the test period will be out-of-vocabulary. This is actually correct behavior — but the design does not discuss how out-of-vocabulary test-period log entries are handled, which could silently degrade the operator log branch without any error.

3. MULTIMODAL FUSION ARCHITECTURE DOES NOT ACCOUNT FOR MODALITY MISSINGNESS. Operator logs are categorical and text entries documenting human actions — these are not continuously sampled. The design encodes the 'most recent 50 log entries within each observation window,' but for machines with infrequent operator interaction, many windows will have far fewer than 50 entries. Zero-padding or missing-token imputation for absent log entries is not equivalent to informative absence — if a machine has no operator log entries, that itself may be informative (automated equipment, or equipment that was not inspected recently). The design does not specify whether missing log modality is modeled as an explicit signal or treated as a missing-at-random imputation problem.

4. VALIDATION WINDOW NOT SPECIFIED. The design describes a test set (random 15% holdout) but does not describe a separate validation set for hyperparameter tuning. Without a validation set, hyperparameter selection (RNN architecture, dropout, window size, TF-IDF dimensions) will occur on the training set via cross-validation or on the test set — neither of which is described. If cross-validation is used on the training set with randomly shuffled folds, the temporal autocorrelation problem applies within the training set as well.

5. RNN COMPARISON BASELINE INCLUDES ONLY SENSOR DATA, NOT OPERATOR LOGS. The design states the baseline is a 'traditional statistical baseline using sensor data alone.' This means the RNN advantage, if any, could be: (a) the RNN architecture is better than the statistical baseline, (b) operator logs add predictive information beyond sensors, or (c) both. These hypotheses cannot be disentangled without a third model: (sensor data only RNN) to isolate the architecture contribution, and (sensor + operator logs, non-RNN) to isolate the modality contribution. The design conflates multimodal with recurrent architecture in the primary comparison.

critic_raw:
This PoC proposes a transformer for multi-class post-discharge complication prediction using clinical notes vs. logistic regression baseline.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE FROM FULL-DATASET FITTING. The design explicitly states: 'preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' The tokenizer and vocabulary built on 100,000 patients including the test set means the transformer has been exposed to the complete clinical language distribution of the study period before any training occurs. For complication prediction from clinical notes, if specific complication types are associated with specific clinical terminology that evolved over the 3-year period (new drugs, new procedure names, updated diagnostic language), the full-dataset vocabulary bakes in that test-period terminology. This is the most consequential leakage point.

2. TEMPORAL SPLIT BUT PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE CONTRADICTS THE TEMPORAL RATIONALE. The design uses a temporal split: 60% training (~22 months), 20% validation (~7 months), 20% test (~7 months of recent data). The rationale is that 'temporal ordering prevents future clinical information from leaking into training.' This rationale is directly contradicted by fitting tokenization, normalization, and vocabulary on the entire dataset including test-period notes. The temporal split prevents target-label leakage but the preprocessing step reintroduces language-level leakage from the exact same future period.

3. MULTI-CLASS COMPLICATION LABEL CONSTRUCTION MAY INTRODUCE VERIFICATION BIAS. Labels are defined as 'the primary or most severe complication' within 30 days. Complications are identified from 'structured follow-up documentation and readmission records.' However, the verification of complications depends on whether the patient returned for follow-up — patients who do not return are labeled as having no complication. This creates verification bias: patients with better access to follow-up care are more likely to have complications detected and labeled. Patients who die at home or seek care elsewhere within 30 days will appear as 'no complication' cases. The design does not address this ascertainment bias.

4. LOGISTIC REGRESSION BASELINE RECEIVES SAME CLINICAL NOTES AS TRANSFORMER — UNFAIR TEXT REPRESENTATION. The design states that 'if structured features are available, these are included for both models.' The transformer receives raw clinical notes. If logistic regression receives the same raw clinical notes, it must use bag-of-words or TF-IDF — a much weaker text representation. If logistic regression does not receive clinical notes (only structured features), then the comparison conflates architecture with modality. The design does not clearly specify whether logistic regression has access to clinical notes and in what representation, which is central to interpreting whether the transformer architecture itself provides value.

5. BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVALS ON MULTI-CLASS AUROC ARE UNRELIABLE FOR RARE COMPLICATION CLASSES. The design reports 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap for the primary metric. For a multi-class complication prediction task where some complications are rare (e.g., rare surgical complications), bootstrap resampling on the test set will produce wide and sometimes degenerate confidence intervals for rare-class AUROC estimates. The design does not specify the minimum class prevalence required for reliable AUROC estimation, nor does it address what constitutes an interpretable result when rare classes have fewer than 50 test-set instances.
